name: Malaysian Nasi Lemak
steps: Cook the rice in the coconut milk.
Fry the fish and serve alongside with the sliced pepper.

ingredients_full.note: standing in for dried anchovies (ikan bilis)

steps_detailed: Cook the jasmine rice in the coconut milk and 0.4 cup water with the torn lime leaf, covered, 15-18 minutes, until tender and fragrant - the coconut milk and lime leaf are what make this rice specifically nasi lemak rather than plain jasmine rice.
Fry the fish until crisp and golden, a stand-in for the crisp-fried dried anchovies traditionally served alongside.
Mash the shrimp paste with the onion and pepper, and fry into a rough sambal-style relish, 5-6 minutes, until fragrant and slightly darkened.
Serve the coconut rice with the crisp fish and sambal alongside.

Nasi lemak's identity is entirely in that coconut-and-lime-leaf-infused rice - dried anchovies (ikan bilis) aren't a distinct product here, so a crisp-fried fish fills the same crunchy, savory role on the plate.
description: Nasi lemak's identity is entirely in coconut-and-lime-leaf-infused rice - considered Malaysia's national dish.

name: Malaysian Roti Canai
steps: Mix the flour, milk, and a little of the butter into a soft, elastic dough. Rest at least 20 minutes.
Stretch very thin, fold repeatedly to create layers, and pan-fry in the remaining butter and oil until golden and flaky.
Serve with a curry or dal for dipping.

ingredients_full.note: beaten, optional, for a roti telur variation

steps_detailed: Mix the flour, milk, a pinch of salt, and a little of the butter into a soft, elastic dough. Knead well, coat lightly with oil, and rest at least 30 minutes, or up to a few hours.
Stretch and slap the dough into a very thin sheet, then fold repeatedly to create many thin layers, and coil into a flat round.

Pan-fry in the remaining butter and oil over medium heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden and crisp with visibly flaky layers - crack an egg into the center partway through for a roti telur variation.
Serve with a curry or dal for dipping - roti canai is a beloved Malaysian breakfast staple with roots in South Indian flatbread traditions.
description: Roti canai, a flaky, layered flatbread, a beloved Malaysian breakfast, likely brought by Indian Muslim immigrants.

name: Malaysian Kaya Toast
steps: Toast the bread.
Spread with the butter and preserves.
Serve with the soft-boiled egg on the side, traditionally cracked into a saucer with soy sauce and pepper.
steps_detailed: Toast the bread until golden and crisp.
Spread generously with the butter and the preserves (standing in for kaya, a rich coconut-pandan jam).
Soft-boil the eggs, crack them into a small saucer, and season with the soy sauce and black pepper.
Serve the toast alongside the soft egg for dipping - a beloved Malaysian breakfast, especially at kopitiam coffee shops.
description: Kaya toast, toasted bread with coconut jam and butter, a Malaysian and Singaporean breakfast staple.

name: Malaysian Char Kway Teow
steps: Stir-fry the shrimp until just cooked, then push aside and scramble the egg.
Add the cooked noodles and bean sprouts, and toss everything together over high heat 3-4 minutes.
Serve immediately.
steps_detailed: Stir-fry the shrimp and garlic in the oil over high heat until the shrimp is just cooked, 2-3 minutes, then push to one side.
Scramble the egg in the cleared space.
Add the cooked noodles, bean sprouts, and soy sauce, and toss everything together vigorously over high heat 3-5 minutes, letting the noodles pick up a slight char.
Serve immediately while hot - a beloved Penang street food classic, best cooked fast over very high heat.
description: Char kway teow, stir-fried flat rice noodles, one of Malaysia's most beloved street foods.

name: Malaysian Nasi Kandar Chicken Curry
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion and turmeric.
Add the coconut milk and simmer 20-25 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Serve over rice.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion, garlic, ginger, and turmeric over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the coconut milk and diced tomatoes, and simmer 22-28 minutes until the chicken is tender and the sauce has thickened.
Serve over rice - nasi kandar curries reflect Malaysia's deep South Indian Muslim culinary influence, especially strong in Penang.
description: Nasi kandar, rice served with a variety of curries, a Penang specialty.

name: Malaysian Laksa
steps: Simmer the coconut milk with the fish sauce and a little water.
Add the shrimp and cook 3-4 minutes until just cooked through.
Pour over the cooked noodles and serve.
steps_detailed: Pound or mince the garlic, lemongrass, and jalapeno into a rough paste.
Fry the paste in a little oil over medium heat, 2-3 minutes, until fragrant.
Add the coconut milk and fish sauce, and simmer 8-10 minutes to let the flavors meld.
Add the shrimp and simmer 3-4 minutes until just cooked through.
Ladle over the cooked noodles and top with the bean sprouts.
description: Laksa, a spicy coconut noodle soup, exists in countless regional variations across Malaysia - real laksa broth is built on a lemongrass-and-chili paste simmered into the coconut milk, not just added at the end.

name: Malaysian Mee Goreng
steps: Brown the chicken, then push aside and scramble the egg.
Add the cooked noodles and bean sprouts, and stir-fry 3-4 minutes.
Serve hot.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground chicken in the oil over medium-high heat, 5-6 minutes, then push to one side.
Scramble the egg in the cleared space.
Add the cooked noodles, bean sprouts, soy sauce, and tomato paste, and stir-fry everything together 3-5 minutes until well combined and heated through.
Serve hot - a popular, spicy-sweet Malaysian fried noodle dish.
description: Mee goreng, fried noodles, a common Malaysian street food with Indian-Muslim influence.

name: Malaysian Beef Rendang

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion and turmeric.
Add the coconut milk and simmer, uncovered, 1.5-2 hours, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has reduced and darkened and the beef is very tender.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Pound or blend the onion, garlic, lemongrass, galangal, and turmeric into a rough, fragrant paste.
Fry the paste in a dry pan or with a little of the coconut milk over medium heat, 4-5 minutes, until deeply fragrant.
Add the beef and brown, 6-8 minutes.

Add the remaining coconut milk, ginger, and kaffir lime leaf, and simmer uncovered over low heat, stirring occasionally, 1.5-2 hours, until the liquid reduces to a thick, dark, clinging sauce that coats the meat rather than a soupy curry - this long, patient reduction is what genuinely separates rendang from a quicker beef curry.
Stir in the toasted coconut in the last 10 minutes.
description: Rendang, beef slow-cooked in coconut milk and spice paste until the liquid reduces to a thick, dark, clinging sauce, is one of the world's most famous Malaysian and Indonesian dishes - lemongrass and galangal are essential parts of the spice paste (bumbu) it's built on, alongside the long, patient reduction that concentrates everything into something far deeper than a simple curry.

name: Malaysian Satay

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Marinate the chicken with the coconut milk and turmeric for at least 20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill until charred and cooked through, about 10-12 minutes.
Blend the crushed peanuts with a little water into a simple dipping sauce.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Marinate the chicken in a little of the coconut milk with the turmeric and lemongrass for at least 20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill or broil, turning, 8-10 minutes, until charred and cooked through.
Whisk the remaining coconut milk with the crushed peanuts, fish sauce, and sugar, and warm gently into a sauce.
Serve the skewers with the peanut sauce.
description: Satay, grilled skewered meat with peanut sauce, is built on a marinade that traditionally includes lemongrass alongside the turmeric - lemongrass's citrusy brightness is a real part of what satay marinade is supposed to taste like.

name: Malaysian Sambal Fried Fish
steps: Fry the fish in the oil until golden and cooked through, 4-5 minutes per side.
Fry the jalapeno and onion in the same pan until softened.
Spoon the mixture over the fish and serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Fry the fish in half the oil over medium-high heat, 4-6 minutes per side, until golden and cooked through, then set aside.
Fry the jalapeno, onion, and garlic in the remaining oil until softened and fragrant, 5-6 minutes.
Stir in the tomato paste and sugar, cooking 2-3 more minutes until it forms a thick, glossy sambal.
Spoon the sambal over the fried fish and serve with rice - sambal, in countless regional variations, is central to Malaysian cooking.
description: Fish fried and topped with sambal, a common Malaysian home dish.

name: Malaysian Ikan Bakar (Grilled Fish)
steps: Rub the fish with the turmeric and garlic.
Grill over medium-high heat, 4-6 minutes per side, until cooked through and lightly charred.
Finish with the lime juice before serving.
steps_detailed: Combine the turmeric, garlic, jalapeno, and half the oil into a paste, and rub it thoroughly over the fish.
Let marinate 15-20 minutes.
Grill over medium-high heat, brushing with the remaining oil, 5-7 minutes per side, until cooked through and lightly charred.
Finish with a generous squeeze of lime juice - a classic Malaysian grilled fish, often wrapped in banana leaf for cooking over charcoal.
description: Ikan bakar, grilled fish, a Malaysian coastal staple.

name: Malaysian Curry Puffs
steps: Brown the chicken and mix with the diced potato.
Make a simple pastry from the flour and water, roll thin, cut into rounds, fill, and fold into half-moons.
Fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion and turmeric, then mix with the diced potato.
Make a simple, firm pastry from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, and roll out thin.
Cut into rounds, fill with the chicken mixture, and fold into half-moons, crimping the edges well.
Fry until deeply golden and crisp on all sides, 4-5 minutes - curry puffs are a beloved teatime snack across Malaysia.
description: Curry puffs, fried or baked pastries with a spiced filling, a beloved Malaysian snack.

name: Malaysian Otak-Otak Style Fish Cake
steps: Mix the finely chopped fish with the coconut milk, egg, and turmeric.
Wrap in foil and steam or bake at 350F for 20-25 minutes until set.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Combine the finely chopped fish with the coconut milk, egg, turmeric, fish sauce, and jalapeno, mixing well.
Wrap portions tightly in foil (traditionally banana leaf, which contributes its own aroma).
Steam over simmering water, or bake at 350F (175C), for 22-28 minutes until firm and set.
Serve warm, unwrapped at the table - a beloved grilled fish cake found in Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia alike.
description: Otak-otak, a spiced fish cake traditionally grilled in banana leaf, a Malaysian and Peranakan specialty.

name: Malaysian Pisang Goreng
steps: Mix the flour with a little water and the sugar into a smooth batter.
Dip the banana in the batter and fry until golden, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, sugar, shredded coconut if using, and enough water to form a smooth, coatable batter.
Dip the banana slices in the batter, coating evenly, and fry in the oil over medium heat, 2-4 minutes per side, until golden and crisp.
Serve warm - a beloved Malaysian fried banana snack, sold everywhere from street stalls to night markets.
description: Pisang goreng, fried banana fritters, a common Malaysian street snack.

name: Malaysian Popiah (Fresh Spring Rolls)
steps: Brown the ground chicken and mix with the cabbage.
Lay the filling and bean sprouts on the tortilla and roll up tightly.
Slice and serve, unfried.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground chicken with the garlic and fish sauce, then mix with the shredded cabbage, cooking briefly until the cabbage just softens. Let cool.
Lay the cooled filling and fresh bean sprouts along the tortilla and roll up tightly into a firm cylinder.
Slice and serve unfried, with a touch of sweet sauce if you'd like - popiah is a fresh, unfried spring roll popular in Malaysia and Singapore, often assembled at the table.
description: Popiah, fresh (not fried) spring rolls, a Malaysian and Peranakan specialty.

name: Malaysian Cendol-Style Coconut Dessert

ingredients.note: cooked, as a cendol jelly stand-in

steps: Warm the coconut milk with the brown sugar until dissolved.
Combine with the cooked noodles in a bowl.
Chill before serving.

ingredients_full.note: cooked, as a cendol jelly stand-in
cooked, as a red bean topping

steps_detailed: Warm the coconut milk with the brown sugar over low heat until fully dissolved, 2-3 minutes, then chill.
Combine the chilled coconut mixture with the cooked noodles (standing in for the green rice-flour jelly strands traditionally used) and the cooked kidney beans.
Serve well chilled, ideally over crushed ice - cendol is one of Malaysia's most beloved cold desserts, especially refreshing in the tropical heat.
description: Cendol, shaved ice with coconut milk and palm sugar syrup, a beloved Malaysian dessert.

name: Malaysian Kuih Bingka (Cassava Cake)

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps: Mix the cassava flour, coconut milk, sugar, and egg into a smooth batter.
Pour into a small buttered dish and bake at 350F for 30-35 minutes until golden and set.
Cool before slicing.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps_detailed: Mix the cassava flour, coconut milk, sugar, egg, and melted butter into a smooth, pourable batter.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 32-40 minutes until golden on top and just set.
Cool before slicing into squares - a dense, chewy cassava cake found throughout Malaysia and the wider region.
description: Kuih bingka, a baked cassava cake, part of the wide world of Malaysian kuih (traditional sweets).

name: Malaysian Ondeh-Ondeh
steps: Wrap the dough around the palm sugar, seal well, and boil until they float.
Roll in the coconut.

ingredients_full.note: ground into flour, or use rice flour

steps_detailed: Mix the glutinous rice flour with warm water into a soft, pliable dough.
Wrap small pieces around a bit of palm sugar, sealing completely, and roll into smooth balls.
Boil until they float, 4-5 minutes, then drain and roll in the grated coconut.

Ondeh-ondeh shares its molten-palm-sugar-center trick with Indonesian klepon - the same technique appears under different names across the region, and the seal has to be complete or the filling leaks out while boiling.
description: Ondeh-ondeh shares its molten-palm-sugar-center trick with Indonesian klepon - the same technique across the region under different names.

name: Malaysian Sambal
steps: Blend the jalapeno, onion, and garlic into a coarse paste.
Fry the paste in the oil over medium heat, stirring, 8-10 minutes until deeply fragrant and slightly darkened.
Cool and store; use as a fiery condiment.
steps_detailed: Blend the jalapeno, onion, and garlic into a coarse paste.
Fry the paste in the oil over medium heat, stirring frequently, 10-12 minutes until deeply fragrant, darkened, and the oil separates slightly.
Stir in the tomato paste and sugar, cooking 2-3 more minutes.
Cool and store in a sealed jar in the fridge for up to two weeks; sambal is the foundational condiment of Malaysian cooking, with countless regional and household variations.
description: Sambal, a broad category of chili-based condiments, a defining part of Malaysian cooking.

name: Malaysian Peanut Satay Sauce

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Simmer the crushed peanuts with the coconut milk, sugar, and fish sauce for 5-6 minutes until thickened.
Serve warm with satay skewers.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Simmer the crushed peanuts with the coconut milk, sugar, fish sauce, and jalapeno if using, stirring frequently, for 6-8 minutes until thickened to a spoonable consistency.
Stir in the lime juice off the heat.
Serve warm with satay skewers, or as a dip for vegetables - the essential accompaniment to Malaysian satay.
description: A standalone peanut sauce, the same sauce that accompanies satay.
